result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before a city wakes up, when the streetlights haven’t yet admitted they’re redundant and the air is still the private property of night-shift nurses, bakers, insomniac poets, and the small brown bats that swoop between the lime trees outside my window. That hour is a pocket universe, folded inside the larger, noisier one most people agree to live in. In it, time is not the brittle, expensive stuff you spend on commute apps and calendar invites; it is a slow-moving syrup, luminous and vaguely hallucinatory, the color of the 24-hour bakery’s neon sign reflecting off wet asphalt. I have been collecting those minutes for years, hiding them like contraband in the lining of ordinary afternoons. They are my unpaid debt to astonishment.

When I was eight I believed the world secretly rebooted every Tuesday at 11:11 a.m. I would stare at the classroom clock, waiting for the slender red second hand to trip over the twin ones, convinced that if I concentrated hard enough I could catch the instant when the sky’s pixels refreshed. I never managed it, but the exercise trained me to notice seams: the way the teacher’s voice sometimes looped half a syllable, the way the playground gravel rearranged itself into perfect tiny ridges overnight. Children are licensed conspiracy theorists; adulthood is mostly learning to apologize for having seen too much. Still, I remain a part-time believer in glitches. Last winter I watched a subway rat drag an entire untouched slice of pepperoni pizza down the uptown tracks, and the impossible ease with which it hauled that greasy cardboard flag made me suspect the station was running on cheat codes. Nobody else looked. Commuters kept scrolling as if the miracle were a pop-up ad they could mute.

I write because attention is a finite resource and I am greedy. Also because language is the only game where cheating is encouraged: you can smuggle oceans into parentheses, smear galaxies across an em-dash, and nobody arrests you for illicit trafficking in scale. The page lets me install new fixtures in reality: a country where the national anthem is performed entirely on tuning forks; a retirement home for obsolete alphabets; a lake so shy it only reflects faces you have not yet worn. The psychiatrists call this dissociation, the priests call it temptation, my grandmother called it “that thing you do instead of visiting.” I call it cartography. If you map the imaginary diligently enough, the real world starts to confess its own improvisations. You notice, for instance, that pigeons walk like they are always rehearsing for a tiny, important parade, or that every hospital corridor smells faintly of iron and forgiveness, a metallic cocktail that stays on the tongue for decades.

Memory behaves like a liquid when nobody is looking. Pour it into a glass and it pretends to be transparent; leave it overnight and it climbs the walls, leaves rings, breeds condensation shaped like the faces you promised never to forget. My father died eighteen months ago, yet he keeps turning up in the wrong footnotes of dreams—last night he was a minor character in a Victorian novel, delivering telegrams in a peaked cap, tipping his hat to the governess who looks suspiciously like my seventh-grade math teacher. When I wake, the room carries a faint after-smell of his menthol cigarettes, though no one in the house has smoked for years. Grief is a hack novelist; it recycles characters, overuses ellipses, insists on sequels. I would complain but the royalties are phenomenal: every time I miss him, a neural pathway widens, a poem earns its keep, a song I hated in 1997 suddenly makes me weep in the dairy aisle because the chorus mentions engine oil and cigarettes and unconditional regard.

We are told to stay present, as if the present were a static country you could immigrate to, rather than a passport stamp dissolving under new ink even as the customs officer raises his fist. I keep trying to bribe the guard with fresh fruit or unsolicited advice, but the border is porous only in retrospect. Tomorrow keeps leaking through the ceiling in the form of alarms, due dates, the incremental browning of the bananas. Meanwhile the past retroactively edits itself: I recently discovered that the scar on my knee—souvenir of a heroic bicycle crash at age ten—was, according to my mother, actually acquired during a tantrum in a supermarket parking lot when I flung myself onto a shopping cart. The discrepancy feels existential. If the wound’s origin story can be swapped out like a faulty battery, what becomes of the superhero origin I constructed around it? Does the child me lose membership in the secret society of kids who court danger to test whether physics obeys passion? Or is the mythology resilient enough to absorb the downgrade from dramatic accident to bureaucratic meltdown? I decide to keep both versions, the way physicists pretend light is both wave and particle, and I carry on limping patriotically through nostalgia’s passport control.

There is, I suspect, a parallel market where unsold time is liquidated in bulk. All those half-hours you spend on hold, the minutes lost while searching for the other sock, the collective centuries burned inside traffic circles—they are compacted into bright plastic bricks and resold to birds. That would explain the unreasonable optimism of sparrows, the way they hurl themselves into hedges as if they have eternities to spare. I keep meaning to file a freedom of information request, to find out whether my lost childhood afternoons have ended up powering some starling’s bender, but the bureaucratic form requires attachments I cannot provide: proof of ownership, a witness signature from someone who saw me exist before I learned to pretend. Instead, I scatter crumbs on the windowsill and call it restitution.

Technology keeps promising to externalize memory, to liberate us from the undignified glue trap of embodiment. Clouds beckon: upload your photos, your documents, the embarrassing sound of your voice cracking at the surprise party, and we will keep them safe, searchable, immortal. But clouds are weather; they migrate, they leak, they indulge in catastrophic mergers. Meanwhile my phone autocorrects “love” to “live,” as if the algorithm has taken a seminar in existential philosophy. I resist the upgrade; I like the typo. It suggests that to commit to one is to insist on the other, that affection is merely existence with better lighting. I imagine a distant future where archaeologists excavate the fossil of my hard drive, decode its strata of grocery lists and half-finished odes, and conclude that our civilization worshipped a deity named Backup, whose anger could only be appeased by periodic human sacrifice in the form of unsaved documents.

Sometimes I dream of a blackout so complete that every screen goes dark at once. In the hush that would follow, we might remember the muscle memory of boredom, the way childhood afternoons stretched like taffy when there was nothing scheduled, no feed to refresh, only the creaking house and the dubious company of your own pulse. I do not hate technology; I mistrust the inflationary universe it sponsors, where every instant must be tagged, shared, monetized, archived, turned into evidence for or against the self. Boredom is now a niche commodity, marketed as mindfulness retreats or monochrome coloring books. We buy it back at a premium after having dumped it on the curb like an embarrassing relative. I want to rescue boredom from the self-help gurus, let it reclaim its dreary throne, because wonder often arrives disguised as tedium’s plus-one.

The other day I watched a woman on the bus apply mascara with the devotion of a nun extinguishing candles. Each swipe was a tiny homily on transformation, on the wager that if you darken what frames the window, the view might cooperate. I wanted to tell her that I, too, perform small engineering feats every morning—reshaping arches, painting over fault lines—just to convince the mirror to keep hosting me. Instead I stared at the emergency exit diagram above her head, where obedient stick figures filed toward an abstract rectangle labeled SALVATION. The drawing has not changed since I was in elementary school; even the hairstyles are retro, a silent reassurance that at least some instructions survive the firmware updates. I imagined the bus tilting into a river, the stick figures sliding off the metal and inflating into actual people, their paper smiles hydrating into terror or joy. Who gets to be the one who opens the exit? Do you need seniority, or is it simply whoever mistrusts the water most?

I used to think growing older would feel like ascending a tower from which you could finally glimpse the mosaic you had only puzzled over in fragments. Instead it’s more like being handed a kaleidoscope someone else has already turned twice. The pieces rearrange whether or not you look, yet you cannot put the toy down because it is wired to your wrist by tendons of obligation and affection. Every so often the chips settle into a pattern so symmetrical it hurts—your mother’s laugh braided with your lover’s scent, the national anthem half-remembered in a foreign airport bathroom—and for a second the design seems revelatory, a proof that your existence was not random. Then the bus hits a pothole, the woman with the mascara gets off at the museum stop, and the colors shuffle again into improbable bouquets.

I have written almost a thousand words and still haven’t mentioned the orange cat who visits my fire escape at dusk, carrying in his whiskers entire weather systems of elsewhere. He refuses to be petted, but he will accept spoken word. I recite grocery lists, failed haiku, the names of governments currently falling, and he listens with the neutral professionalism of a therapist paid in kibble. When he leaves, the sill holds the ghost shape of his warmth, a temporary amendment to the architecture of solitude. I would like to think he takes something of my sentences with him, perhaps a syllable that rattles inside his skull like a lone dice in a cup, ready to land on some later night when the moon is particularly outrageous and a voice across the alley happens to pronounce exactly the right vowel. A borrowed phoneme could be the tipping point between feral and domestic, between the rooftops and the ragrug by someone’s hearth. Language as adoption papers, unsigned.

The word count creeps toward its contractual ceiling, the digital equivalent of the polite cough that ends office parties. I feel the urge to stash one last paragraph under the mattress, a secret annex where future insomniac selves can find it and remember that once, at 3:42 a.m., I still believed attention could be a form of devotion, that to watch the city yawn itself awake was to participate in a rehearsal for mercy. Outside, the first garbage truck is backing up, beeping its Morse code that translates roughly to: whatever you did not finish will still be here tomorrow, only heavier. I believe it, the way I believe bread rises because someone lingered in the warm kitchen, the way I believe the bats will circle again, mapping darkness with small leathery sonar hearts.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
